does: This paper introduces a scenario-grounded benchmark called Scientific Discovery Evaluation (SDE) that assesses large language models on their ability to conduct authentic scientific research across biology, chemistry, materials science, and physics. Rather than testing decontextualized factual knowledge, the framework evaluates models at two levels: question-level accuracy on scenario-specific items and project-level performance requiring hypothesis generation, experimental or simulation design, and result interpretation. The authors apply this two-phase evaluation to state-of-the-art LLMs and reveal significant performance gaps compared to traditional science benchmarks, showing that model scaling provides diminishing returns for scientific discovery tasks and identifying systematic weaknesses shared across top-tier models from different providers.
connects: This work responds to a critical limitation in existing science benchmarks, which typically measure isolated factual recall rather than the iterative reasoning, hypothesis-driven investigation, and evidence interpretation central to actual scientific practice. It builds on prior efforts to develop more challenging domain-specific benchmarks but extends these by grounding evaluation in genuine research projects defined by domain experts, moving beyond static question-answering toward dynamic scientific workflows. The paper engages with ongoing questions about LLM capabilities in reasoning and knowledge application, building on work examining whether scaling and reasoning techniques genuinely improve scientific understanding versus superficial pattern matching.
enables: This benchmark framework enables more rigorous evaluation of whether LLMs can serve as genuine research assistants or merely simulate scientific language. By establishing performance baselines on authentic scientific tasks, it opens pathways for targeted development of model capabilities in hypothesis generation, experimental design, and evidence interpretation. The identified systematic weaknesses across models suggest specific areas for future investigation: improving scientific reasoning under uncertainty, enhancing integration of domain knowledge with logical inference, and developing better mechanisms for theory-driven rather than data-driven predictions. The framework itself is extensible to additional scientific domains and could support the development of specialized models and training approaches specifically optimized for iterative scientific discovery workflows.
